being achieved by the story. And so, you know, the four big things that come out of a story are a transformational change in the person themselves. So the person goes from being timid to bold, fearful to courageous, bored to passionate and enjoying life, misguided to guided, evil to good, et cetera. A value can be an idea. The story leads us to a profoundly important idea. A value can be a behavior. So the story gets us to a specific change in the way we behave toward a situation. Or the story gets us to a benefit outcome, something we got from it. And so you start to organize these things by category. And these are some of the categories you might use. By chronology, so what order they occurred in, timelines, and by purpose, the punchlines, how you might use them. And you may very well have the same one in three places, four places, five places, or you may have it only in one. So a principle of purpose, number nine now, is trying to figure out, if you look at page 77, what the purposes are that a story might serve and or how the story might be modified, tweaked, re-engineered, engineered backwards in order to fit these purposes. So here's like, and I do it mentally now, I don't do it on paper, but this was originally my purpose list. And so, and your list might be different, by the way, probably is different. But so one kind of universal purpose of stories is to explain yourself, to signify and symbolize what you're about, who you are, what you want to be known for. And even corporations have these, not just personal. And so, Nordstrom's has a whole collection of frequently retold stories that tell the employee and the customer what Nordstrom's is all about. And so there's a Nordstrom story about the guy who got the wrong tie and is halfway across town in a hotel and calls Nordstrom's and they rush across town with his tie. There's those kinds of stories that are cultural about Nordstrom's. FedEx has stories about FedEx people who have braved hurricanes, you know, on and on and on. And so one purpose of the story is to concisely explain who you are, what you're about, what you want to be known for. Another is to gain authority. Why are you here? Why should I listen to you? And remember, this is not a resume. This is a story. And you make a point. So like I tell, and I've always used it with corporate clients ever since I got it. But like I have the Weight Watcher story I tell, which sometimes I identify by name and sometimes I don't, but of the CEO calling me into his office and closing the door and saying, you know, I figured out that we're paying you 10 times more per hour than we're paying me. And my answer being, well, there's a good reason for that. You know how to do everything there is here at this company except one thing, which is how to get a customer. And without knowing how to do that, everything else you know how to do really doesn't have any value. And that's the one thing you don't know how to do. So it's perfectly appropriate for me to get paid 10 times more than you do, but we'll keep it our little secret. Now, I tell that story and I've always told it to corporate clients. Right? Why? Because it hits, it heads off the issue of fee number one. So it addresses money and it clarifies exactly what it is that I'm bringing to the table. Right? I know how to put a customer in your place and you are clueless about that. Right? So it's an authority story to compress time and quickly establish backstories. So if you have a long complex story about how you got from point A to point B, you need to rewrite it in a way that compresses time to convey empathy, to prove that you and I are alike somehow. Now, this is real important like if you're a genericist selling to a niche, if you're a niche selling generically, if you're crossing economic demographic boundaries. Right? So the more unlike you are, the more you need a story that somehow makes you and I alike. Right? So that there's some point of connection. Another one is a story about our united enemy. One of the tables was talking about that, you know, during the last exercise that everybody's got. Every industry has somebody they hate. Every group of people has somebody that they sort of blame all of the ills that they experience on. And so a very easy way to bond with them is the common enemy. If you sell financial stuff, the higher up the effluency ladder you go, the more they hate the IRS. And so it's even more useful to have a, I hate the IRS story and here's why. If you're selling to multi-millionaires, then if you're selling to middle income people, they actually don't hate it as much. To make yourself human and therefore believable, so personal flaw story, to present what outcalls an inconvenient truth. Right? So if you have to give them some piece of bad news, like you were talking about Stephen having to deal with the inevitable person who comes to Quantum Leap who has written a book for which there is no market and has printed 50,000 of them in her garage and is emotionally married to that book, you know, while one approach of course is to say, Mary, your book has no market, you know, and I hope you live in a cold climate so that you have a fireplace because they'll come in real useful, right? And here's the seven reasons why they have, it has no market and here's the 17 ways you fucked the whole thing up and here's where you have to start, right? This is not as effective, right? It has telling Mary a story, right? Either personal experience or about somebody else who has gotten themselves into that same fix and either refused to embrace the truth and therefore wound up broken, living under a bridge and suicidal, etc., or embraced the truth and came out the other end, right? With great success and happiness and fame and fortune. And you are more likely to get some traction with the story than you are with the direct confrontation. And I will sometimes air air air in a setting like that with direct confrontation and usually, you know, in a one-on-one setting you can do, but usually it doesn't work out well. It works out like when I did that at Dottie-Walter seminar with the person out in the hall crumpled up on the floor weeping and three other people consoling her and everybody else, you know, thinking I should be burned at the stake. So better I should have told a story. I did. I hope you live in a warm or in a cold climate because they'll come in real handy. And that's exactly what I said. As a matter of fact, turns out she lived in California to present philosophy, to teach or glorify a value that we believe in. So like if you want to preach and sell discipline back to you, you need some discipline stories. You need some that teach the virtue of discipline and you need some that glorify discipline. So you need those in your arsenal. Obviously, to teach or glorify a value, link to them buying what we are selling. All right. And so on a real simplistic level, that's the, you know, poor people have big TVs, big people, rich people have big libraries. You know, that's a way of getting at the value of investing in books and tapes, which of course is what we want you to do. You could embellish that. And actually, Jim has an embellished version of it about going for the first time he was ever in a millionaire's home and being taken into the library and having the person explain to him that poor people have big TVs, rich people have big libraries. And so there's a story version of that. To teach or glorify a value we want or need conformance with, we need a certain behavior from them. Or to teach or glorify a value that's contrary to their past or to their typical thinking. All right. So everybody who teaches old fashioned sales, right, teaches prospecting, has some story. Because most people view rejection as bad. And most people don't want to be rejected. And so their premise coming into being taught how to prospect is, if I get a lot of rejection, I get away from whatever the hell is causing me all the pain and agony and unpleasantness and rejection, right? We want them to value rejection. Because if they're going to co-prospect, they're going to get a shitload of it. And they need to survive it. So every sales trainer has some story that presents the law that gets to the point of law of averages that every no moves you closer to a yes. You celebrate the no's because you're one step closer to the yes. And so every sales trainer has some story about that. The really unimaginative ones usually use Edison and the umpteen number of experiments required to eventually invent the light bulb, which doesn't really resonate with salespeople. But just about every sales trainer uses that story. But some Frank Betker, of course, who was a baseball player, uses the baseball analogy. But just about every sales trainer with assault has some story to deal with the fact that you need to be happy about having people slam doors in your face and throw gasoline on you and scream at you.
